reasoning: The base rate of formal declarations of war in the post-WWII era is extraordinarily low, as most conflicts are fought without such legal declarations. India and Pakistan have not formally declared war on each other since 1971. In May 2025, an unprecedentedly severe military crisis took place between the two countries, involving missile strikes on military bases and cities (the most intense use of force since 1971). Despite the severity of this crisis, neither country issued a formal declaration of war. A US-brokered ceasefire was implemented around May 10/11, 2025, and subsequent analyses throughout June and July refer to the conflict in the past tense or as an ongoing diplomatic issue rather than an active traditional war. Since an event of this magnitude did not trigger a formal declaration of war, the threshold for doing so is evidently almost impossibly high. Therefore, the probability that either nation will issue a formal declaration of war by the end of 2025 is effectively at the minimum possible threshold.
